On 2 February 2005 the applicant’s wife, Mrs Józefa Krasuska, informed the Court’s Registry that the applicant had died on 30 December 2004. She stated that she wished to continue the proceedings before the Court in her late husband’s stead.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1934. He died on 30 December 2004. He lived in Zielona Góra, Poland.

In July 1994 the applicant and his wife concluded a contract with E.W. and W.W., the owners of a company W. Under that contract the company was to carry out building work on the applicant’s house. On 24 November 1994 the applicant, considering that the company was in breach of the contract, withdrew from it.

On 1 February 1996 the applicant and his wife (“the plaintiffs”) sued E.W. and W.W. in the Zielona Góra Regional Court (Sąd Wojewódzki), seeking compensation for the serious damage to the house allegedly caused by the defendants.

On 13 March and 27 June 1996 the court held hearings and heard evidence from witnesses.

On 24 July 1996 the court instructed Poznań Technical University (Politechnika Poznańska) to prepare an expert report. The report was submitted to the court on 4 November 1996. The defendants challenged the report on 2 December 1996.

At a hearing on 23 December 1996 the Regional Court ordered the experts to revise their report. On 26 January 1997 they upheld their original conclusions.

On 17 July 1997 the plaintiffs modified their claim.

The court held a hearing on 29 October 1997.

On 31 March 1998 the court ordered the experts to supplement their report.

The parties filed their pleadings in March, April and October 1998.

On 4 November 1998 the court held a hearing. The Government maintained that in the course of the hearing the plaintiffs had modified their claim. The applicant contested this.

On 19 November 1998 the court gave judgment and dismissed the claim. The plaintiffs appealed.

On 18 May 1999 the Poznań Court of Appeal (Sąd Apelacyjny) quashed the first-instance judgment and remitted the case to the Regional Court.

On 30 September 1999 the Regional Court held a hearing. It decided to obtain a fresh expert report. In the meantime, the applicant had made two unsuccessful applications for his claim to be secured.

On 20 February 2000 the expert report was submitted to the court.

At the hearing held on 9 May 2000 the court heard evidence from the expert.

The Government submitted that on 16 May 2000 the plaintiffs had altered the amount of the damages they had sought. Subsequently, they applied for an exemption from the court fee due for the increased claim. On 12 October 2000 the court refused their application. That decision was later upheld by the Poznań Court of Appeal. The applicant denied that they had altered their claim.

On 28 February 2001 the plaintiffs again unsuccessfully applied for an exemption from payment of the court fee for the increased claim. Eventually, on 16 March 2001, the particulars of that claim were returned to them on account of their failure to pay the fee.

The Government maintained that at the hearing held on 8 May 2001 the plaintiffs had increased their claim and had stated that they would not seek an exemption from the court fee. However, on 4 June 2001 the particulars of the amended claim were again returned to them on account of their failure to pay the fee in question.

The applicant stated that this was not true. He maintained that, in a pleading of 11 May 2001, after the experts had again revised their valuation of the damage sustained, he had indeed extended the amount of the damages sought to 300,000 Polish zlotys (PLN), mostly because he had been forced to do so by the court. He had also sought an exemption from court fees since to do otherwise would have been illogical.

A hearing scheduled for 12 July 2001 was adjourned at the plaintiffs’ request. A subsequent hearing was held on 6 September 2001.

On 4 October 2001 the court heard evidence from the parties and closed the trial. On 11 October 2001 it gave judgment, awarding the applicant PLN 50,000 plus statutory interest.

On 30 November 2001 the applicant appealed against the judgment. Shortly afterwards, on 10 December 2001, the Regional Court ordered him to rectify the formal shortcomings in his appeal. On 8 January 2002 the applicant applied for an exemption from the court fees for lodging the appeal. The Regional Court rejected his application on 23 January 2002. The applicant unsuccessfully appealed against the refusal; eventually, he paid the court fees on 22 February 2002.

On 20 June 2002 the Poznań Court of Appeal heard, and dismissed, the applicant’s appeal. Since the applicant refrained from lodging a cassation appeal (kasacja) with the Supreme Court, the judgment became final on that day.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 92/03) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Andrzej Pisk-Piskowski (“the applicant”), on 13 November 2002.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr J. Wołąsiewicz, of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

On 24 March 2004 the President of the Fourth Section decided to communicate the complaint concerning the monitoring of the applicant’s correspondence to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it was decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1967 and lives in Opole, Poland.

A. The criminal proceedings

In 1998 the prosecution service filed with the Opole District Court (Sąd Rejonowy) a bill of indictment in which it charged the applicant with making unlawful threats. In the course of the court proceedings the applicant was represented by a court-appointed lawyer. On 6 November 2001 the trial court held a hearing at which the applicant and his court‑appointed lawyer were present. During the hearing, the court scheduled the next hearing for 4 December 2001 about which the applicant was informed.

On 4 December 2001 a hearing took place before the trial court. The applicant did not attend the hearing but his counsel was present. The applicant failed to give any reasons for his absence and the trial court finished the proceedings and decided in the presence of the counsel that the judgment would be delivered on 11 December 2001.

On 11 December 2001 the Opole District Court delivered a judgment in which it convicted the applicant as charged and sentenced him to a prison term of one year and six months. Neither the applicant nor his counsel was present at the delivery of the judgment.

The applicant did not appeal against the judgment of 11 December 2001 and did not apply for a copy of the reasons of that judgment.

Subsequently, the applicant lodged an application for leave to apply for a reasoned judgment out of time. In his application, he argued that his absence at the hearing held on 4 December 2001 was justified because he had been recently released from the detention and on 5 December 2001 he had been assaulted. The applicant also complained that he had not been informed about the date of the delivery of the judgment. On 9 April 2002 the Opole District Court examined the application and dismissed it. The court, in a reasoned decision, established that the applicant had known about the hearing scheduled for 4 December 2001. He failed to attend it without providing any justification for his absence. Therefore, the trial court was entitled to terminate the proceedings in his absence on the basis of Article 376 § 2 of the Code of Criminal Procedure. The court further examined the reasons for the absence submitted by the applicant. It established that in fact he had been released on 22 November 2001 - two weeks prior to the hearing. Moreover, while it was true that the applicant had been assaulted, nevertheless, that had happened the day following the hearing and the injuries sustained by the applicant, according to a hospital certificate, were not serious and the applicant was not admitted to hospital. With regard to the complaint that he was not informed about the date of the delivery of the judgment, the court found that the applicant had not been diligent since he had not contacted his lawyer. The applicant was aware of the scheduled hearing and knew the address and the name of his court‑appointed lawyer. The applicant appealed against this decision. On 16 May 2003 the Opole Regional Court (Sąd Okręgowy) examined the reasons given by the District Court and upheld the decision.

On 14 January 2002 the applicant started serving the prison sentence ordered by the judgment of 11 December 2001.

B. The monitoring of the applicant’s correspondence

On 6 December 2002 the Court received the applicant’s first letter dated 13 November 2002. The letter was sent while the applicant was serving a prison sentence in the Wrocław Detention Centre. The envelope in which the letter was delivered bears the following stamps: “District Court in Legnica, censored on 22.11.02” (Sąd Rejonowy w Legnicy, cenzurowano dnia 22.11.02) and “252, 14 NOV 2002, register number 2738/01” (252, 14 LIS 2002, numer ewid. 2738/01).

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 63391/00) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mrs Jadwiga Marszał (“the applicant”), on 7 July 1999.

The applicant was represented by Mr J. Zaleski, a lawyer practising in Katowice, Poland. The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agents, Mr K. Drzewicki and subsequently Mr J. Wołąsiewicz , of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

On 13 February 2003 the Court decided to communicate the application to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

The applicant was born in 1954 and lives in Bytom, Poland.

A. Proceedings for compensation and reinstatement

The applicant worked as the head of the sales department in a coal mine “Rozbark” in Bytom. On 24 August 1994 she was dismissed from her job.

On 2 September 1994 she lodged a claim for compensation and reinstatement with the Bytom District Court of Labour (Sąd Rejonowy Wydział Pracy). Before 26 January 1995 the trial court held four hearings and heard evidence from one witness.

On 2 March 1995 the parties reached a friendly settlement and the District Court discontinued the proceedings. On 16 March 1995 the applicant’s lawyer asked the court to resume the proceedings, as the defendant company had failed to comply with the terms of the settlement. On 27 June 1995 the Katowice Regional Court (Sąd Wojewódzki) quashed the decision of 2 March and resumed the proceedings.

On 21 September 1995 the District Court held a hearing. On 23 October 1995 it stayed the proceedings until the conclusion of criminal proceedings against the applicant (referred to below). The applicant’s further appeal against that decision was dismissed by the Katowice Regional Court of Labour on 28 December 1995.

On 28 January 1999 the applicant asked the court to resume the proceedings. On 25 March 1999 the court held a hearing. On 29 October 1999 it dismissed her application. On 29 December 1999, on the applicant’s appeal, the Katowice Regional Court resumed the proceedings.

Between 9 March and 5 December 2000 the court held five hearings and heard evidence from five witnesses. On 5 December 2000 the Bytom District Court gave judgment. The court granted the applicant compensation and dismissed her claim for reinstatement. Both parties appealed.

On 8 May and 5 June 2001 the Regional Court held hearings. On 15 October 2001 the applicant sent a letter to the President of the Katowice Regional Court asking for a hearing date to be set.

On 21 November 2001 the Regional Court dismissed the defendant’s appeal. 20 December 2001 the Regional Court gave judgment and dismissed the applicant’s appeal. The judgment is final.

B. Criminal proceedings

On 30 December 1995 the Bytom District Prosecutor (Prokurator Rejonowy) submitted to the Bytom District Court (Sąd Rejonowy) a bill of indictment against the applicant and three other employees of the “Rozbark” coal mine. The applicant was charged with carrying out fraudulent financial operations. On 14 March 1996 the Bytom District Court returned the case-file to the District Prosecutor instructing him to complete the investigation. The District Prosecutor appealed. On 3 April 1996 the Katowice Regional Court (Sąd Wojewódzki) ordered the District Court to proceed with the case.

During the proceedings the District Court held fifteen hearings and heard evidence from several witnesses.

On 23 September 1998 the Bytom District Court delivered a judgment and acquitted the applicant. The District Prosecutor appealed.

On 8 February 1999 the Katowice Regional Court quashed the first-instance judgment and remitted the case to the prosecution authorities for a further investigation.

On 10 July 1999 the District Prosecutor ordered an expert opinion to be obtained. The expert submitted his opinion on 22 October 1999. On 2 August 1999 the Bytom District Prosecutor decided to stay the investigation until an expert on management prepared an opinion.

On 15 December 1999 the applicant sent a letter to the Regional Prosecutor complaining about the slow progress of the proceedings. On 20 March 2000 she sent another letter to the Minister of Justice (Minister Sprawiedliwości) asking about the progress of the investigation proceedings. In a letter of 14 March 2000 the Bytom District Prosecutor informed the applicant that there were difficulties in finding an expert competent to prepare an opinion.

On 30 April 2001 the expert on management submitted his opinion to the Prosecutor. The investigation was resumed on 2 July 2001.

On 28 December 2001 the District Prosecutor submitted to the Bytom District Court a bill of indictment against the applicant and three other persons.

On 28 August 2003 the Bytom District Court discontinued the criminal proceedings against the applicant.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 37293/09) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Jerzy Zawisza (“the applicant”), on 10 July 2009.

The applicant was represented by Ms M. Gąsiorowska, a lawyer practising in Warsaw. The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr J. Wołąsiewicz of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

The applicant alleged that the lustration proceedings in his case had been unfair.

On 10 May 2010 the President of the Fourth Section decided to give notice of the application to the Government. It was also decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1943 and lives in Warsaw. He is a retired professional soldier.

On 11 April 1997 the parliament passed the Law on disclosing work for or service in the State’s security services or collaboration with them between 1944 and 1990 by persons exercising public functions (ustawa o ujawnieniu pracy lub służby w organach bezpieczeństwa państwa lub współpracy z nimi w latach 1944-1990 osób pełniących funkcje publiczne; “the 1997 Lustration Act”). It entered into force on 3 August 1997. Persons falling under the provisions of the 1997 Lustration Act, i.e. candidates or holders of public office such as ministers and members of parliament, were required to declare whether or not they had worked for or collaborated with the security services during the communist regime.

In 2002 the applicant became involved in politics. In 2004 he intended to stand as a candidate in the elections for the European Parliament on behalf of the Self‑defence party. However, the party withdrew its support for the applicant after he informed it that during his military service he had collaborated with the counter-intelligence agency.

At the end of 2004 the Institute of National Remembrance drew up a list of officials, collaborators and candidates for collaborators of the communist security services. In November 2004 the list was published on the Internet and unofficially named “the Wildstein list” after a journalist who had allegedly removed it from the Institute. The name “Jerzy Zawisza” was on the list and the applicant asked the Institute whether it concerned him. The Institute informed the applicant that it did not concern him.

The applicant was elected a Member of Parliament in the elections held in September 2005.

On 9 October 2005 the applicant in his lustration declaration stated that he had not collaborated, served or worked for the communist-era security services. On 12 October 2005 he informed the Speaker of the Sejm that he had had contacts with the military counter-intelligence.

On an unspecified date the Commissioner of Public Interest (Rzecznik Interesu Publicznego) informed the applicant that he had doubts as to the truthfulness of his lustration declaration and invited him for an interview on 10 October 2006. At the next interview the applicant was invited to read the records concerning his alleged collaboration.

On 21 December 2006 the Commissioner applied to the Warsaw Court of Appeal to institute lustration proceedings against the applicant on the grounds that he had lied in his lustration declaration by denying that he had collaborated with the secret services.

On 28 December 2006 the Warsaw Court of Appeal decided to allow the Commissioner’s request and instituted lustration proceedings against the applicant.

On 17 and 24 January 2007 the applicant’s lawyers consulted the classified part of the case file in the secret registry of the court. On both occasions the lawyers were entitled to take notes in the special notebook kept by the Head of the secret registry. However, they did not avail themselves of that possibility.

On 7 March 2007 the Warsaw Court of Appeal, acting as the first‑instance lustration court, found that between 1964 and 1975 the applicant had been an intentional and secret collaborator of the military counter-intelligence service and had therefore submitted an untrue lustration declaration. One judge from the panel of three expressed a dissenting opinion. The judgment was served on the Commissioner of Public Interest, the applicant and his lawyer.

The applicant lodged an appeal in which he maintained, in particular, that as a member of the armed forces he had been ordered to collaborate with the military counter-intelligence agency and that, therefore, his collaboration did not fall within the ambit of the Lustration Act. Alternatively, he argued that when submitting his declaration he had made a justifiable error. According to the Government, the applicant’s appeal did not concern the issue of restrictions on access to the case file.

On 19 September 2007 the Katowice Court of Appeal, acting as the second‑instance lustration court, upheld the impugned judgment.

The applicant lodged a cassation appeal against the judgment. The Government submitted that the applicant had not invoked the issue of restrictions on access to the case file in his cassation appeal.

On 1 December 2008 the Supreme Court dismissed the cassation appeal as manifestly ill-founded. The decision was served on the applicant on 19 February 2009.

The applicant is prevented from standing in elections and from holding other public functions for a period of 10 years.

According to the Government, in the course of the proceedings before the Katowice Court of Appeal and the Supreme Court neither the applicant nor his lawyer availed themselves of the possibility to consult the case file or to take notes of it.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 4317/04) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Andrzej Kaźmierczak (“the applicant”), on 22 December 2003.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr J. Wołąsiewicz of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

On 18 October 2005 the Court decided to give notice of the application to the Government. It also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1955 and lives in Wrocław.

On 18 April 2002 the Opole District Court (Sąd Rejonowy) ordered that the applicant be remanded in custody in view of the reasonable suspicion that he had committed several offences of fraud and that he had organised and led a criminal group.

On 15 July 2002 the court extended the applicant’s detention until 17 September 2002. It relied on the reasonable suspicion that the applicant had committed the offences with which he had been charged and on the risk that he might tamper with evidence.

On 13 September 2002 the court extended the applicant’s detention until 17 November 2002. It repeated the grounds previously given for his detention and found that his remaining in custody was necessary to complete the expert psychiatric examination as to the state of the applicant’s mental health.

The applicant’s detention was subsequently extended on 14 November 2002. The court attached importance to the serious nature of the offences with which he had been charged and the likelihood of a heavy sentence being imposed on him.

On 11 December 2002, the applicant was indicted on several charges of fraud while acting in an organised criminal group. Subsequently, in March 2003, the case file was transferred to the Wrocław-Śródmieście District Court for reasons of procedural economy.

On 23 April 2003 the Wrocław-Śródmieście District Court extended the applicant’s detention for a three-month period, until 23 July 2003. It considered that the severity of the anticipated sentence and the need to secure the proper course of the trial justified the continuation of that measure.

On 22 July 2003 the Wrocław-Śródmieście District Court extended the applicant’s detention until 23 October 2003. It also observed that the applicant’s continued detention was necessary in order to secure the proper conduct of the proceedings, given the gravity of the anticipated sentence. In addition, it referred to the complex nature of the case.

On 13 October 2003 the Wrocław-Śródmieście District Court extended the applicant’s detention until 23 January 2004. The court referred to a strong likelihood that he had committed the serious offences with which he had been charged and stressed that a heavy penalty might be imposed on him. It also observed that no other measures would prevent the applicant from obstructing the proceedings or going into hiding.

That decision was upheld by the Wrocław Regional Court (Sąd Okręgowy) on 7 November 2003. The Regional Court stated in the relevant part of the reasons for its decision that: “The appeals are not well-founded. (...) The District Court correctly pointed out that the fear that the accused would tamper with evidence arises also from the fact – as proved by the collected evidence - that he organised and led the group of offenders. Consequently, there is a strong probability that by using the same methods of pressure on the co-accused he may induce them to tamper with evidence.”

Between 14 November 2003 and 12 January 2004 the court held four hearings, two of which were adjourned.

On 21 January 2004 the Wrocław-Śródmieście District Court extended the applicant’s detention until 23 March 2004.

In the course of the investigation and the court proceedings the applicant made numerous unsuccessful applications for release on bail or under police supervision and appealed, likewise unsuccessfully, against decisions extending his detention.

On 18 February 2004 the Wrocław-Śródmieście District Court dismissed the applicant’s application for release, holding that the grounds originally given for his detention were still valid. The Court also expressed an opinion that his detention could be lifted and replaced by another preventive measure. It fixed bail at 100,000 Polish zlotys (PLN) [approx. 25,500 euros (EUR)], to be paid before 10 March 2004.

On 22 March 2004 the Wrocław Śródmieście District Court extended the applicant’s detention until 10 April 2004. It also stated that the detention would be lifted if the applicant put up the bail.

On 25 March 2004 the applicant was released on bail. The court found that releasing the applicant on bail would be sufficient to secure the proper conduct of the proceedings, taking into account the fact that it had heard evidence from him.

Between 9 March 2004 and 20 March 2006 the court held thirty‑three hearings.

It appears that the criminal proceedings are still pending.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 35916/08) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Grzegorz Korgul (“the applicant”), on 15 July 2008.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr J. Wołąsiewicz of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

The applicant complained, in particular, that he had been deprived of access to the Supreme Court.

On 23 July 2010 the President of the Fourth Section decided to give notice of the application to the Government. It was also decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time (Article 29 § 1).

In accordance with Protocol No. 14, the application was allocated to a Committee.

The Government objected to the examination of the application by a Committee. After having considered the Government’s objection, the Court decided that the case should be examined by a Chamber.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1962 and lives in Lublin.

On 6 June 2007 the Lublin District Court convicted the applicant of several robberies and sentenced him to 3 years and 6 months’ imprisonment. By a judgment of 28 December 2007 the Lublin Regional Court upheld the first‑instance judgment.

On 4 January 2008 the applicant requested the Lublin Regional Court to grant him a legal-aid lawyer for the purpose of lodging a cassation appeal with the Supreme Court.

On 7 February 2008 a legal-aid lawyer was assigned.

By a letter of 14 March 2008 the lawyer informed the court in a one sentence-long opinion that he had not found any grounds on which to prepare a cassation appeal. This statement did not include any reasons for the refusal to lodge a cassation appeal.

By a letter of 17 March 2008 the court forwarded the lawyer’s statement to the applicant. The letter read as follows: “...The Lublin Regional Court informs you that your legal-aid lawyer, advocate Mr W.W., informed the court in writing that he had not found any grounds to prepare a cassation appeal. In consequence, the time-limit for lodging a cassation appeal by a lawyer of your own choice expires on 7 April 2008.”

The letter as well as the opinion were served on the applicant on an unspecified date.

On 27 March 2008 the Lublin Regional Court refused to assign a new legal-aid lawyer to the applicant. The court stated that because the previous legal-aid lawyer had not found any grounds on which to draw up a cassation appeal, the mere fact that the applicant disagreed with this decision could not require the verification of this opinion by successive lawyers.

1.0: The applicant, Mr. X, sought protection under the Fourth Article of the European Convention on Human Rights, claiming that the detention order was unlawful as it failed to take adequate precautions to ensure the applicant's right to a fair trial. The finding of the Domestic Authority appeared to have disregarded the fact that the witness required special protection following the reception of intimidation threats. A careful examination of the case law of other domestic tribunals had revealed that alternative measures to guarantee the witness's safety would have been more easily acceptable. Accordingly, the European Court of Human Rights quashed the detention order and found that the Domestic Authority had not taken sufficient measures to allow the witness to give evidence securely.

"The European Court's decision in the case of Smith v. United Kingdom, which concerned discrimination against an English individual due to their disability, has highlighted the need for greater accessibility in public spaces. The court's emphasis on the importance of inclusive design principles underscores the ongoing efforts to reform infrastructure and services to cater to diverse demands of the public."

"An examination of the judgements issued by the European Court of Human Rights shall reveal a consistently applied standard regarding the balancing of individual rights with the rights of the state. The intricate complexities surrounding these interindividual cases underscore the imperative of thorough scrutiny in the realm of human rights."

"On a recent judgment delivered by the European Court of Human Rights, the Court unanimously held that failure to provide reasonable support for a visual arts project under the Locus Standi Regius application does not have the effect of excluding jurisdiction for the examination of the case, citing Article 17 of the European Convention on Human Rights despite initial reservations regarding the applicability of the case. The applicants successfully argued that the lack of financial support led to a situation where 'reasonable persons' in the public sphere might have had difficulty engaging with their work due to the high cost, which triggered a reasonable expectation to explain the source of funds used for display in a publicly accessible premises. The ruling followed extensive examination of relevant precedents prior to establishing the relevance of a balancing of interests concerning artistic freedom against public space requirements."

According to the European Court of Human Rights, a direct marketing campaign that includes personal data processing without the clear stipulation of consent renders unlawful under Article 8 of the Convention, yet, it is not an absolute prohibition.

The European Court of Human Rights examined a landmark case originating from Glasgow, Scotland, where a mother had demanded the United Kingdom reconsider its stance on the ban of smoking in workplaces citing Article 11 of the European Convention on Human Rights. However, the first time the European Convention on Human Rights emphasized individual freedoms was in a 1959 seminal judgement when two Jehovah's witnesses were forced into military service under British law, thereby compelling them to violate their deeply-rooted faith, citing Article 9. The court ultimately ruled in favor of the applicants, citing Article 6 – the right to fair trial.

The recent decision in the case of Kudla v. Russia, a significant victory for the right to private and family life, underscores the need for governments to ensure that policies and laws do not disproportionately intrude upon the most intimate aspects of human experience.

The European Court of Human Rights has made recent rulings on several cases in which individuals have claimed that the UK government's strict austerity measures have led to a lack of access to adequate medical care, notwithstanding the country's obligation under the European Convention on Human Rights to ensure the right to medical care.

The European Court of Human Rights has once again affirmed the principles of Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights, rejecting the government's claims that a defendant's access to mental health services was hindered by the failure to provide adequate accommodation.

The European Court of Human Rights has repeatedly confirmed that the right to freedom of expression is not absolute. While Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights protects this fundamental right, it also provides Member States with the discretion to impose limitations under the European Convention on Human Rights for the sake of public safety or given the 'serious substance' of reason.

The case of Ozdil Korkmaz v. Turkey (App. No. 24597/03) is one of the most complex and poignant examples of the European Court's efforts to balance individual rights with state interests. In this landmark case, the applicants, two Turkish citizens, brought forth allegations of State infidelity, with regard to their children, with clear indications that the Turkish government's refusal to allow in the family members of foreign husbands of Turkish citizens had left them at the mercy of unpredictable marriage and separation laws that operated only in their favor. With a backdrop that conjures up a dystopian vision of individual freedom suspended, a world ruled solely by State institutions, can it be truly said that anyone in Turkey is protected from state-driven forces that seek to constrain individual rights, or are the interests of the powerful and the fortunate able to easily transgress against those of the poor?

The applicant, a British citizen, challenged the domestic authority of the United Kingdom under the European Convention on Human Rights, asserting that she had suffered inhuman treatment at the hands of the police.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld the right to freedom of expression, recognizing its cornerstone role in enabling individuals to engage with democratic processes and participate in the development of public opinion, a cornerstone of a functioning democracy as outlined in Article 11 of the European Convention on Human Rights.

The Court of First Instance in the Administrative Tribunal saw justification in upholding the claimant's right to freedom of expression under Article 10 ECHR in this instance, as the original ruling had not provided sufficient context prior to making the determination.

"The European Court of Human Rights today ruled against Ireland in a landmark case involving an individual who was temporarily detained for questioning without access to a lawyer due to the perceived threat they posed to national security, citing Article 15 of the European Convention for Human Rights as a precedent while stressing that the necessity to act to protect public safety does not waive the individual's fundamental right to a fair trial and prompting a wide-reaching debate about the balance between individual freedoms and the need for law enforcement to prioritize public safety."

"If the applicant, a Hungarian national, had not been awarded the sum of 12,500 euros in her main claim and an additional 11,000 euros in her consequential damage claim, she would be considered to have suffered a total loss of 23,500 euros, which would be disproportionate in comparison to the allegedly breached right and a disproportionate fine would be imposed as per the ECHR's jurisprudence in the matter of X v. Y versus Council of Europe case. The fine set under domestic law was not subject to a meaningful political vote by the Czech parliament; it was imposed by the Supreme Administrative Court without adequate reasons.

The finding of the European Court of Human Rights in the landmark case of X v. Lithuania that the state's failure to translate public services into the language of a citizen who has suffered gender-based discrimination has not equalized the prospects in the relevant aspect of life, is reminiscent of a precedent set by the Court in Steklova v. Ukraine twenty years prior.

The European Court of Human Rights has ruled that a prolonged detention of 23 days without access to familial or informational contact constitutes a violation of Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The court found that the restriction on her attempt to support her partner's migration process constitutes a breach of this fundamental right.

"The Commission asked the Fourth instemntry Member State why, despite its previous guarantees, the applicant family had not yet received the necessary support to facilitate the prompt reunification of the minors in Strasbourg, given that this situation seemed to be causing them disproportionate distress and was thereby allegedly violating article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights, the Committee noted that the third-party entities intervening in this case had expressed concerns regarding the family's safety in the event that the family were to be relocated, however, in answer to this the Commission pointed towards their efforts to ensure a smooth relocation as such in order to protect and ensure the integrity and full safe and wholesome upbringing of all members of the family.

"The European Court of Human Rights, pursuant to Article 17 of the Convention, reiterated its long-held position that the Hungarian authorities had undermined the right to free assembly by suppressing the nationwide student-led demonstrations in Budapest in 2006, leading to a significant deterioration in peaceful protest rights for students in Austria. Observers at the hearing noted a widening chasm between the state's handling of civil society groups advocating for reform and the traditional established government-funded agencies, thus casting doubt on efforts to maintain open, transparent dialogue in Europe's public forums."

"As the ECHR considered the case of M.C. v. United Kingdom, it clarified that the fundamental right to freedom of expression under Article 10 of the ECHR is not absolute and that the restriction of this right must be proportionate to the legitimate aim pursued, thus balancing the plausibility of the restriction with the potential impact on the exercise of freedom of expression – the Court noting that this needs to be based on a clear and lawful basis, as arbitrary interferences may undermine the statutory human rights framework."

"The European Court of Human Rights' recent decision in case of X v. Y has reignited debate regarding the extent of an individual's right to anonymity in high-profile cases, amidst concerns that greater disclosure could stigmatize vulnerable claimants and compromise the integrity of investigative processes."

"In the wake of the 2004 Istanbul convention, the European Court of Human Rights established the necessity of safeguarding the right to freedom of assembly in order to counterbalance the increased restrictions imposed upon civic protests in several European states, notably in Greece and Turkey, where the ECHR has repeatedly ruled against the authorities for enforcing laws which resulted in the physical suppression of collective gatherings."

"The application of Article 2, section 1 of the European Convention on Human Right by the government has been brought into question yet again in today's landmark case, as the petitioner contesting the construction of the new prison in the town of Ravensburg due to alleged inadequate conditions for housing the mentally ill, cites Article 3 of the Convention, concerning 'protection from inhuman and degrading treatment, punishment or fines in any circumstance regarding the above article.'"

The Strasbourg Convention on Human Rights, being a cornerstone of European judicial framework, holds that states must ensure effective remedies for breaches of any ECHR provisions, particularly Article 11's guarantee against arbitrary extradition of individuals.

The decision in the case of Andrews v United Kingdom was handed down on 21 November 2000 in the First Instance Chamber, and the Court by an equally divided Court delivered its Judgment on 21 November 2000. The applicant, Andrew Andrews, is a United Kingdom national who suffered severe psychological harm as a result of the criminal investigation into his father’s death. The applicant complained that there had been no sufficient or satisfactory alternative to detention to satisfy the Article 5(1)(e) ECHR standard, particularly during the limited periods of his detention after the interview sessions at the Post Mortem and the preliminary inquiry where his father’s body was found.

"In the landmark case of Smith v. ECHR, the applicant alleged that the detention of M. Smith, a foreign asylum seeker, in a secure facility for 5 years, was in breach of Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights, which safeguards the right to respect for private and family life. Prior to the detention, the UK authorities had received no substantial grounds to justify his decision, and subsequent detention orders were based on incomplete information. Moreover, the applicant's lack of response to the asylum application, it was noted that all asylum seekers have the right to prompt attention from any asylum seeker with unverified claims of persecution or non-prosecution within a specific time frame of 2 months. Hence, the House questioned to balance the security of the asylum seekers' public order to the breach of his fundamental right under right by keeping him incarcerated for prolonging more than the given period was rendered unwarranted. Notably, the Court's propensity for issuing binding judgments shapes the conduct of national governments and their agencies, and, consequently, guides the advancement of human rights reform.

In determining an individual's right to freedom of expression, the ECHR emphasized that it was a fundamental aspect of human dignity and a cornerstone of contemporary democratic societies. The protection of this right, however, is not absolute, and limitations on it were permissible when such limitations were in accordance with the provisions of the European Convention. These limits primarily targeted the rights and freedoms that might infringe the interests of others, such as the rights of third parties, public morality, and order and national security. In the former instance, the court often scrutinized the scope and nature of the prohibited information to ascertain whether such limitations safeguarded the values and rights of the third parties. In cases where a limit sought to protect public morality, the court considered the impact on the recipient audience, focusing on avoiding undue offense and avoiding the diffusion of hate speech. Where the limitation promoted national security or order, the court weighed the burden on the right to freedom of expression against the security concerns, in balancing the dual evils of terroristic dissemination on the one hand against societal security on the other. The European Court of Human Rights, in its landmark decision in the case of Olsson v. Sweden, held that a country's obligation to combat racism and xenophobia requires it to take positive measures to prevent and combat discrimination based on nationality, ethnicity, or national origin. The court found that Sweden's failure to implement effective measures to protect specific ethnic minority groups, including Roma and others at risk, constituted a breach of Article 14 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The court emphasized that effective measures to combat discrimination require not only the prohibition of discriminatory conduct, but also the promotion of measures with a preventive and proactive approach.

The European Court of Human Rights, seated in Strasbourg, France, handed down a landmark decision in the case of X and Y, two individuals from Lithuania, who complained that their detention by Lithuanian authorities, awaiting an deportation hearing, constituted a breach of Article 14(1) of the European Convention, as the detention did not satisfy the requirement for reasonable grounds based on presumed guilt as laid out in Article 7(2).

The court found that the applicant's actions did not amount to a violation, as they had been driven by a legitimate purpose, namely to protect the embryo, and taken all reasonable steps to preserve the embryo while also respecting the life and bodily integrity of the mother."

The European Court of Human Rights has ruled that states have an international obligation to ensure that individuals with disabilities have equal access to online public services, including voting systems, although exceptions may be necessary in exceptional circumstances. The court's decision followed a case involving a voter with a visual impairment who sought a preliminary measure requiring the UK government to make electronic voting systems available in accessible formats. While the ruling encourages Member States to proceed with digitizing voting systems, it also cautions against imposing blanket obligations on states to make all voting systems accessible digitally. This represents a significant shift in the court's stance on digital accessibility for voters with disabilities, highlighting ongoing debate over balancing necessary accommodations with potential security concerns.

"In a landmark case before the European Court of Human Rights, the applicant, Ms. X, brought a complaint alleging violations of her rights under Article 8 of the European Convention. The European Court of Human Rights recently ruled in a landmark case that states have a duty to provide sufficient support and resources to ensure the effective rehabilitation of minors found guilty of crimes, considering the harm caused to them and the severity of the offense.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld the rights of individuals who have been subjected to arbitrary detention while in pre-trial detention, as affirmed by the landmark case of _Omnium Sanitarium Alpbach v Austria_. In a decision that has resonated throughout the continent, the court ruled that a patient's conviction for having failed to comply with a healthcare regulation after their release from a psychiatric hospital was not a valid reason for imposing a custodial precautionary measure that transgressed the right to liberty enshrined in Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights.

"In the case of X, Y v. the Republic of Z, the ECHR reiterated the long-standing principle that the State's duty to protect the right to private life extends not only to physical security but also to the virtual sphere, as seen in the recent ruling over the interception of a citizen's personal communications. However, the court also acknowledged that the limitation on this right, justified under Article 8(2), must be proportionate to a legitimate aim, such as maintaining national security, and must be carefully weighted against the potential impact on the individual's liberty."

"The admissibility of a piece of evidence in a human rights case before the European Court of Human Rights turned on the delicate balance between the need for a more comprehensive understanding of the suspect's past actions and the imperative to prevent the re-victimization of the Claimant. The Court's nuanced approach to such evidentiary issues is a hallmark of its commitment to upholding human rights, even in the face of morally complex and emotionally charged cases, thereby reinforcing the importance of the Rule of Law in safeguarding individuals' fundamental freedoms."

The European Court of Human Rights issued a significant ruling in the case of 'Case from Bulgaria', where the court found that the Bulgarian government's policy of separating children from their parents at border crossings constituted a breach of Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The court stated that the separation was not proportionate to the supposed security risks posed by the family members, and that the Bulgarian authorities should ensure that all measures taken to stem irregular migration flows relied on human rights safeguards. Furthermore, the court emphasized that measures to expedite asylum applications for migrants should be implemented with due regard for the best interests of the child.

The principle of non-discrimination, as enshrined in Article 14 of the European Convention on Human Rights, requires that public authorities ensure equal treatment of individuals within their jurisdiction, without distinction on any ground such as sex, ethnic or national origin, colour, race or social and economic circumstance.
20.0: The European Court of Human Rights ruled that the police's reliance on biometric data to identify suspects may violate article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights, in a landmark case that has sparked debate on the use of surveillance technology in law enforcement.

The European Court of Human Rights issued a landmark ruling in the case of Smirnov v. Belgium, affirming the applicant's rights under Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The judgment, delivered on 5 December 2022, concerns the applicant, Vitali Smirnov, who was detained without any effective supervision for 45 days despite multiple applications to the authorities.

The European Court of Human Rights, in its landmark decision in the case of Olsson v. Sweden, held that the country's juvenile justice system's practices violated Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights regarding inhumane treatment.

"The Court, by a decision delivered on 13 February 2023, struck down a specific clause of the Law no. 100/1997 pertaining to Protection of Private Life, implemented by the Republic of Bulgaria, as it violated Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights; the Court held that the government's right to conduct intelligence operations to counter terrorism cannot justify the unreasonable restriction on individual freedoms to expect, however, an overriding need in the specific circumstances; in a related case, the Court ruled that State oversight required before implementing such operations and stricter criteria for assessing the necessity and proportionality inherent to each individual case."

The European Court of Human Rights, aware of the precarious livelihoods of journalists operating in wartime zones, has established a binding precedent by ruling that the suppression of press freedom infringes upon the rights enshrined in Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights; however, the manner in which this right is exercised must remain proportionate to the threats posed by the circumstances of the situation, thereby striking a delicate balance between the need for security and the duty to provide the public with an informed discourse on matters of public importance.

"The European Court of Human Rights was established in 1949 to protect the fundamental rights and freedoms of individuals, as enshrined in the European Convention on Human Rights. The European Court of Human Rights, in its landmark decision in the case of McLaughlin and Breslin v. the United Kingdom, the ECHR reinforced the notion that the right to effective remedy is inextricably linked to the protection of human dignity, a fundamental principle enshrined in Article 3 of the European Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms.

The European Court of Human Rights, the guardian of fundamental human rights in Europe, recently ruled in a landmark case [Case No. 58296/21] that governments must ensure adequate healthcare facilities in prisons to prevent the spread of COVID-19, despite claims that increased healthcare demands on an already stretched system. The ruling, based on Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights, acknowledged that failing to provide such facilities would amount to inhuman and degrading treatment.

"In the landmark case of Karner v. Germany, brought before the European Court of Human Rights in 2003, the court interpreted Article 11 of the European Convention on Human Rights in a manner that would have far-reaching implications for social organizations and private individuals alike. The petitioner, a former footballer, claimed that the German Football Association's rules preventing her from wearing a knee ligament band in a demonstration of solidarity with injured players were in breach of her right to freedom of expression enshrined in Article 11. The court ultimately ruled in her favor, paving the way for a reevaluation of the boundaries of sports regulation and free speech."

The European Court of Human Rights hearing today was an unprecedented opportunity to set a crucial precedent for the protections of individual freedoms in the digital age. The case centre-piece involved a high school student who was denied entry to her school's science fair due to her anti-Israeli slogans on her social media profile. The complainant argued that the school's actions constituted a violation of the freedom of expression under article 10 of the European Convention. The court ultimately ruled in her favour, citing the importance of balancing the right to free speech with the need to maintain a respectful and inclusive environment.

The European Court of Human Rights has extensively ruled upon the relationship between Article 2 of the European Convention and the rights of individuals to information on the investigation and prosecution of crimes. A notable case that stands out is the 'Ratnikov v Russia' judgment, wherein the court held that Russian Federation's breach of Article 2 by failing to give the applicant timely disclosure of the identity of the person responsible for the act was a fundamental breach of his right to justice. The expansive ruling served to establish a higher threshold for States to meet in regards to disclosure obligations of their investigating authorities, illuminating the complexity surrounding balancing investigative processes with individual rights.
